content:
First, recall that every even perfect number has the form  
  Eₚ = 2^{p–1}(2^p – 1)  
where 2^p–1 is prime (a Mersenne prime).  We seek all positive n such that  
  n^n + 1 = 2^{p–1}(2^p – 1).  

Compute n^n+1 for small n and compare to the first few even perfects:

 n  |  n^n + 1   |  even perfects Eₚ − 1  
----+------------+--------------------
 1  |    2       |  6−1=5, 28−1=27, 496−1=495, …  
 2  |    5       |  (matches 6−1=5?)  → 5≠2^2·3=6  
 3  |   28       | 28 = 2^2·7       → perfect!  
 4  |  257       |  496−1=495 < 257 < 8128−1=8127  
 5  | 3126       |  496−1=495 < 3126 < 8128−1  
 6  | 46657      | 8128−1=8127 < 46657 < 33550336−1  
 7  | 823544     | 33550336−1 much larger  
 …  

The only match is n=3, since  
 3^3 + 1 = 27 + 1 = 28 = 2^2·7  
and 2^3−1 = 7 is prime.  No other small n work, and no odd perfect numbers are known.  Hence the only solution is

\boxed{3}.
